speaker: interviewer
text: And we're about to begin tape 2 here on May 3rd, 1995-- interview with Mrs. Lilly Tykocinski. Mrs. Tykocinski, you, you mentioned that you were able to get and receive letters from your brothers in the labor camps. What did, what did they say about their lives in the labor camps in these letters?

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't say much. Everything is fine. We are doing well. We are working. And we are healthy. And that's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just-- we-- when we wrote to them-- and we sent packages, so if we put money in, we sewed it in a jacket. And when I wanted to make it known to him where it is, I write like dyslexia-- reversed. You know? Backwards. That's how I wrote to them where it is. And they understood me. We had a code like that. Because if you write in the normal language, they will check it out.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we had letters. We sent out letters to them every week, every day, whenever we could just to let them know how we are doing. And they wrote maybe once a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned you sent money, a jacket sometimes. What other kinds of things did you try to send them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food. Food. Whatever we could spare and put together. We sent them food in the packages and warm underwear and things like that when the winter came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever any way of knowing if they got the food or the clothing or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They acknowledged it. They acknowledged it. Somehow this was-- they were building, they were building for the Germans either the tracks or, or some other things, some other camps. So the Germans needed them for the production, what they need to-- you know, to have [NON-ENGLISH] working for them, Jewish people working for them, young people-- all the young people were there. My brother was at that time 17 or 18 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Now, you, you also mentioned that your part of Poland was treated differently than other parts of Poland, politically.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were annexed. They called it the Dritte Reich. We were the Anschluss to the Dritte Reich. So we had food, cards, food stamps, like they have it here. And with the food stamps, you can go to the store. And you had a ration. You had so much meat, so much bread, so much other products for every family. And that's why-- and there was the factory. And everybody worked in the factory. And the old people, the sick people, whoever couldn't, they, they made a, a selection. They sent them away. We didn't even know where and when. The ghetto was crowded. People were dying. But it wasn't as gruesome like in Warsaw, because they treated us-- maybe because they had the Gestapo or the commission coming and taking pictures to show for the world that it wasn't so bad, maybe they kept us as a, as a symbol or something to show off with us. And the factory that we worked, they needed because they had the uniforms that we camouflaged for the winter. We painted them white when they went to Russia. They should blend into the snow. And in the summer, they were the print-- the green, leafy print. So that's what we worked on it. And they needed it. So it was a big production going on. And there was one German who was very nice to the Jews in Zawiercie, maybe a little bit as nice like Schindler, because he protected those few people, you know. He, he received gifts, but he was very nice. And he said he needs the factory. He needs the people. And he kept us till the last minute. Almost all Poland was liquidated, and we were still in the ghetto and working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Yeah, he survived. And friends of mine, you know, went to court. And they said good things about him. I don't remember his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you working in one of his factories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was one factory--

speaker: interviewer
text: One?

speaker: interviewee
text: --but a big one, one big one. And I worked there, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your exact responsibility?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was making the hats, the soldiers' hats, changing them from left to right. You know, so I was sewing on a sewing machine. I learned how to sew on a sewing machine. And I, and I was making the outside, the band outside of the hat, of the German hat. And, you know, you had to make like, 200, 300 a day. And I worked from the morning till the evening. But at least we came back into the ghetto. We felt a little bit safer, protected by somebody [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till 1943, you know, till August, that they surrounded. We heard that there will be a liquidation of the ghetto. That this is it. This is the end. They cannot keep us anymore. We, we heard the news.

speaker: interviewee
text: So everybody prepared themselves. They said that, that Wednesday the 26th will be the liquidation of the ghetto. So my father-- somebody came to my father, if he knows how to get out of the ghetto, if he knows Polacks some place to run. My father said yes. And he says, you're coming with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He just decided to leave the family and take me with him. And at night, the ghetto was surrounded already. We were all ready because we knew there was nowhere to go. We didn't have any papers. And I wouldn't risk it. I says, whatever's going to happen with, with my mother, with my father, will happen to me. And my father took me and, and this man. And we walked in the ghetto. And there was a big wall, like a, wire fence. And the man jumped up to jump over. There was the first shot. I said to my father, I'm not going. And I left him there. He didn't come home. And I didn't know where he went.

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked home, 12 o'clock at night. And I met the Gestapo walking. And they asked me, what are you doing? But there was the Jewish commander from the police. And he says, go home. He knew me, because they knew if they-- why should they kill me now? I'm going tomorrow, anyhow, to, to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we, we were surrounded the whole night. And the next morning, everybody walked out from the house taking whatever you can take. Thousands and thousands of people, because I don't know how many we  were there. And we assembled on one place. And then everybody was starting going out to the gates. They says, take everything off what you have, your watches, your rings, whatever you, you carry, your money. And put it in the bag when you went through, through a gate to go into the wagons, to the open, to the closed cars. So everybody threw in everything. And we walked.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother went, my sister, and the baby-- a nine-month-old baby, my older sister. And I had a friend, a boyfriend and his sister and younger brother. And we all, like, stuck together. And we all walked out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when it came to go into this wagon, my mother couldn't get up. It was so high. And the Germans with the dogs were behind you and beating you and screaming, get in, get in. So my friend went up. And he took the baby. And then he-- I pushed my mother up, not to be hit, not to be killed before. And we all got into the car, maybe 100 people in one car. You couldn't breathe. The doors closed, and we start rolling. We didn't know where we going. Nobody believed that we going to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, if you're young, you don't think. I, I only said, what's going to be with the end of me? What kind of dead will I have? What, what's next? My older sister was worried. She was very pessimistic. She understood the German during the war. And she listened to the radios. And she heard Hitler's speeches. And people came and asked her. And she was so-- she knew she was not going to survive. She knew from Polacks that they were building Auschwitz. And they are burning Jews. I didn't know that. She never told me this. She was so withdrawn from life. She didn't care about anything, even in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were throwing out-- I had something left. We were throwing out everything through the window, through a little window like that from the wagon not to take it to Auschwitz till we arrived in Auschwitz. And the wagons opened, and everybody out. And my sister's holding the baby. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] And my mother says, I'll take the baby. [CRYING] Oh, I can't [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we didn't know where we going. And there were all the SS and the dogs and so many people. And they sent the men separate and the women separate. And my sister says, Mother, I'll carry the baby, Mama. She says, I'll carry the baby. [CRYING] Then my mother took the baby and says children, save yourself. I'm going to go. She took the baby. This is the last picture I have seen of her walking into a truck, to a truck. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They pushed them into a truck. This was the last thing that I have seen of my mother with the nine-months-old baby getting into a truck. And we three remained. And then we walked to Birkenau. We didn't know where we going. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So we came into a barrack. Take everything off. So we got undressed. Put everything away, whatever you carry. I was holding onto my toothbrush like to dear God. Everything, even the toothbrush. I had big, long braids, as you see here on the picture. They opened them up if there is nothing hidden in the braids, you know, gold or, or diamonds. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So they opened up my hair. And we were standing naked-- completely, just, you know-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And they tattooed our arms. And this was my name for the last, next two years, 56065. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were tattooed according to ABC, so I was Butnick. I was from the early people. My sister had 69, I think. She was in line with me. Can you see it? Finished?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then they told us to walk through the street completely naked. I didn't know if I should put my hair to the front or to the back or to hide myself. Men were walking around. We were completely regarded as-- completely we lost all our dignity. They took away all the dignity from us. We didn't know what's going on. And then we went-- the men were shaving us with some stuff. And then we went into the sauna. And we didn't know what's going to happen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we showered. And after the shower, they told us to walk out of there for clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got a evening dress, a black. My sister had a red baby dress. And my other sister had some kind of another child's dress, my older sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: They shaved our hair. So when she put a machine into here, completely to a zero, completely to a zero. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't know how I looked. But when I looked at somebody else, I couldn't recognize anybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I had my-- I, I put on my dress. We didn't get any underwear, any shoes, god forbid, anything-- just a dress to cover, to cover the body. And I looked at my sisters. I couldn't recognize them. So I started calling the names. And like a hysteria came over us because we couldn't recognize ourselves. So we started laughing, hysterically laughing how we looked.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then [SIGHS] they put us into a block. We didn't know where we going, what block, what's going to be. And up on the [INAUDIBLE], on top. 10 people-- five in one side, and five in the other side. We slept like, like, like sardines, one into the other. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was the first night. And we didn't know which block. And we didn't know what's going on and what's happening. Then the next morning, 4 o'clock, everybody out in the street. So this was for, for Appell, for, for counting five in a row all along. Everybody had to stay to be counted. So we could stay for five hours till everybody was counted.

speaker: interviewee
text: People were losing their mind. People were going to the electric wires because they couldn't take it, what's going on with us. If you either-- you walked around at night, and you didn't know where you are and what's going on, the, the guard was shooting you. So deads were coming in from left and right. We were standing on the Appell and being counted. If somebody was missing, you stayed for hours over there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: After the Appell, there was outside, behind the blocks there was-- they called it the [NON-ENGLISH]. There was an empty space, a big space. And the ground was lime. You know what's lime? When it's wet, it's, it's gluey. It's not sand. And when it's dry, it's hard-- lime.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were staying on this [NON-ENGLISH] till 5 o'clock in the evening like animals-- just dressed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We got a little [INAUDIBLE] in the morning. They gave you a pot and a red dish. And you had to carry this with you wherever you go. And we stood on this [NON-ENGLISH] the whole morning, not knowing what's going on and what are we going to do and how-- no bathroom, no water, no drinking, nothing-- just sitting and talking. You walked around, you were afraid you're going to get lost. You will not know where to get back to which block. [DRINKS] The next day, again the Appell. And people were pushing and shoving and screaming. So there is a block over-- this was the head of the block. And she had a secretary. And there was some people that were working, you know, cleaning up the, the place.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the secretary-- we were screaming-- not me, but the people in general. And she said to us-- she was a Jewish Slovakian woman-- you pigs. You screaming-- turn around and see the, the chimney, how the chimney is burning, that your mothers and fathers and children are burning in there. We didn't know, because nobody told us what's going on. So everybody got very quiet. And we turned around. And the chimney was black, going up in smoke. And then we, we start smelling the flesh. And then when we realized, she says, your mothers and fathers and children are dead. Then I realized that I lost my mother because they says they're taking away the mothers and the children in a different place. So I says, now this is the end. So every day, we were watching, every night how the chimney was burning all the people. Every day, transports were coming. And were burning-- the chimneys were burning. And we were going on the [NON-ENGLISH] and coming back to the block rain or shine, every day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the people-- there were almost every second day selections. Whoever fell, fell. They took you away. Whoever they didn't like, they put away. Whoever survived, survived. I was walking down from this upstairs down. I was stepping every morning on deads. Here was somebody that I knew. Here was somebody older that died, a mother of some, of a friend of mine. You know, coming down, you didn't know every morning, where are the deads.

speaker: interviewee
text: So life-- we-- I was on, on the quarantine they called at Birkenau-- quarantined for four months. Normally, people did not survive four months, because either you survived two, three weeks, and they took you to work right away, or you were dying or you were dead. You went to, to, to the oven. They kept us four months there, from August 26 till Christmas. And we were going through hell.

speaker: interviewee
text: The winter came. They gave us the wooden shoes from Holland. That's why I bought a pair of Holland shoes, and I keep it in my car for a souvenir. And we walked, and they were rubbing our feet because this was not comfortable. And then when it was raining, those shoes got stuck in the lime. And we couldn't get them out. We came up on the-- on our bed, there. I have to say bed. I don't know how to explain this-- the [NON-ENGLISH] they call it. And the shoes and the feet were with lime. They were drying out at night. We were to scrub off the, the dirt from the night. We didn't wash. We didn't brush our teeth. We didn't have-- we had two meals a day. In the morning, a little [NON-ENGLISH]. And then in the evening, a, a portion of bread. And maybe they gave you a tiny little salami liverwurst and, and the little soup that was like the dirty water when you wash the dishes. That was black water with maybe one bean or something in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the food that we lived on. And people were fighting for it. And I was just, just, what's gonna be the next? What's gonna be my end? How will it feel to die? Or, or how will I ever die?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the winter came. And we didn't have warm clothes. They gave us warmer dresses in the stripes and the shoes. And we were staying day in and day out on the Appells in the morning. And it was cold and frozen. And somebody was missing. And we stood maybe for five hours, completely-- the people were falling down freezing from the cold. I was holding my older sister next to me. And she was-- when after the countdown was done, they let us in into the barracks, I lay down on top of her. With my body, I warmed up my older sister. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I went to see if my other sister-- she was in a different block already-- to see if she survived because she-- while sitting on this [NON-ENGLISH], on this lime, she developed some kind of infection. So she went to the [NON-ENGLISH]. This is the hospital. And somehow she-- somebody liked her there, the, the nurse or, or the doctor. And they cured her. And they begged her to stay. They will let her clean the, the hospital. She should stay. So she says, no. She wants to be with her sisters. And she came out. They didn't bring her back to our block. She went to another block. And then was, was the selection. And she didn't make it. Right before that, this was the most-- was a selection-- the selections were going on every, every day. You never knew what. For entertainment we had a selection. So Mengele was there. And there was a selection. Nobody knew if the right or the left is good.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I says, what am I going to do? I have my two sisters. And my two sisters were completely helpless. My oldest sister was 20-some years. She was helpless because she lost the baby. And she had the milk coming in because she was nursing. And it was painful. And I didn't know what to do. So some older woman says to tie her breasts. Anyhow, she suffered with this plenty. It's like she gave up. So I divided my sisters between every third, fourth person in a, in a single line to face Mengele because he was going to the right and to the left and to the right and to the left. And my two sisters went to the right. And I was the last one.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he hit me, like, over the head here with a stick. He was sitting on an edge of a table, of a desk with the, the stick here or here. And he hit me with the stick over to the right. And I came to the [INAUDIBLE]. And I said, I have my two sisters with me. But I didn't know if  it's the good side or the bad side, because there was no sign. But later on, we found out it was the good side. But this hit, I felt for years. Even today, I feel the-- the stick over my shoulder because he just went by the neck-- this way by the neck or this way by the neck. Mengele.

speaker: interviewer
text: He didn't speak at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was just sitting with one foot, like-- with one foot on the edge of the desk and going this way or this way. He didn't speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: A good-looking [NON-ENGLISH], a good-looking young [NON-ENGLISH]. Who looked at him? We were afraid to look. I didn't know if I'm going to look it will be good. If I'm not going to look it will be good. I just walked over like, like a nothing, you know, to, to get through. And I got my-- all, all of us. We, we survived this.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you walked over. How, physically, did the selection process work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Naked. You had your clothes under the arm. And you just walked. And he was looking at you. To the left, to the right. To the left, to the right. Just like that. Everybody was naked. And he looked at  you, doing what he liked or whatever. That's why I said to my sister, don't-- let's not go all three of us together. Maybe he'd pick two to the right and one to the left or whatever. I, I figured it out. And we were lucky that time that we all three came out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you first got to  Auschwitz, they separated the men from the women.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the women. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were not able to see your father after that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father did not go with our transport. He was hiding. Later I found out. Because the men in the factory kept maybe 40 people still in the factory to work for him-- professionals. And my brother remained there. And my father went to his brother. And they had a basement with my brother with his family. And they were hiding in the basement for another day, because he thought maybe they'll survive, you know. The basement wasn't prepared, really, to survive the Germans. But he just-- he says to Auschwitz, I have time to go. He left us. This was the last time I have seen him when I said goodbye to him that evening. And my brother says the second day, they took them all out from the bunkers, from the basements. And they sent them to Auschwitz the next day or two days later. I have never seen my father any more. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was with Mengele. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then it was going closer to the, to the, to the winter, to November, December. And I was separated somehow. There was a selection. And everybody had-- we had lice [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by the millions. They were eating us alive. This was right-- maybe after a week being there from, from all the dirt. So everybody was getting sick. And everybody was bitten from the lice. And everybody was scratching. Everybody had, like, crust, you know, on the body. They took us once in a while to the sauna. They disinfected the clothes. And they let us wash up. But you put on the dirty clothes back on. But the lice didn't disappear. This was eating us alive for three months. So the last selection was everybody had a very unclean body from the scratching, from all the dirt. So my sister, my older sister did not get through the selection. I went through, somehow, the selection with, with the scratches.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she didn't go through. She went to a different block. And I remained here and some of my friends. And maybe two days later, I went to see if she is there. She wasn't there, my oldest sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hela. My older sister from me, Menya, was on a different block when she came back from the [NON-ENGLISH], from the sickness. [SIGHS] Gosh. And there was a selection. And they asked, who wants to go to work? That's what she told me. And she was-- she says, I was so weak. I didn't know what kind of work. So she went to the selection. And there is a Block 10 that everybody was waiting to go to the oven. [SIGHS] I have no way to put this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was winter. It was snow. And I went to, to the Block 10 to the  window to call her name, to see her. And she came to the window. This was the only block that had bars in the window. And she says, give me some water or snow. So I gave her a pack of snow through the window. And she said to me-- I have to say it in Polish because it sounds so good. And then I'll translate it to you in English. [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what did she say?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH] This means, with my clear mind, I'm going to die. And alive, I'm going to be burned. That's what she said. Save yourself if you can to be able to tell the story what happened. And that was the last time I have seen her. This is the one that was so pessimistic, you know, that she couldn't-- she, she knew everything. That's why she says to me, alive, they're going to burn me. And with my clear mind, I'm going to die. Then I was left alone. It was the last selection. They were picking to go to the factory to work in the factory. I was chosen. I'm the lucky one from all the rest of us. This was by December, Christmastime. I go to work. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: [DRINKS] This, this is still 1943?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. To go to work for the factory. Like after New Year's, I'll go to work to the factory. I was chosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a half hour? Whew. We covered a lot of stuff, yeah? This is four months only.
